app.name: Token Design
app.tagline: Frame your token — without blind spots.

hub.title: Frame your token, without blind spots.
hub.subtitle: Answer a few plain-language questions. The assistant infers what you're building, which regulatory box it falls into (EU — MiCA / MiFID II), what that requires, and the launch checklist — points of no return included.
hub.lifecycleNote: Token Design helps at Conception — high-level framing. “Build an app” is a parallel track (authority & custody), not a stage.
hub.disclaimer: This tool structures, educates and flags — it is not legal or financial advice. EU framework (MiCA / MiFID II). Have every decision validated by counsel.

create.title: Frame a token
create.desc: Design a new token — or diagnose an existing one. Identity → regulatory class → red flags → launch checklist.

analyze.title: Analyze a token
analyze.desc: Diagnose an existing token with the same engine, in audit mode.

build.title: Build an app
build.desc: An app that acts on users' assets: authority scale → risk profile + custody checklist (XRPL).

stub.body: This angle is being built. The deterministic engine that powers it already lives in {engine} and is unit-tested.

create.forkSub: First fork: the nature of your project drives everything else.
create.resetConfirm: Reset all answers and start over?
create.startHint: Start setting the axes to see your likely class.
create.alsoBuild: Also building an app that acts on users' assets?

deeper.title: Next steps in the suite
deeper.intro: Token Design frames your token at a high level (identity, EU class, key XRPL / legal impact). To dig into each dimension, continue in the dedicated apps:
deeper.canvasDesc: Web3 lean canvas — often the upstream starting point (Grounds).
deeper.tokenomicsDesc: Design supply, distribution and value capture (Tokenlab).
deeper.note: Separate apps in the suite. Not legal or financial advice.

create.autresP1: Building an app that acts on users’ tokens (read, propose, authorize, deposit) belongs to the authority-over-assets layer: how much power you take over the user’s wallet, with what reversibility, who holds the keys.
create.autresP2: This part will be covered in the full version. The MVP covers creating a token.
create.b1Sub: Your token’s identity. If you’re unsure on a question, pick “I don’t know” — we’ll take the prudent assumption and flag it in the dossier.
create.b2Sub: What triggers the watch points and shapes the checklist.
create.alsoPlausible: On a class boundary — also plausibly: {classes}. We retain the heaviest; have it confirmed.
create.rfNone: ✓ No major watch point detected among the rules covered by the MVP.
create.legal: Reminder: structuring, not legal advice. Non-exhaustive list, EU framework, to be validated with counsel.
create.sec3sub: Steps filtered by your class. The points of no return require everything upstream to be locked first.
create.alsoAppT: Your project also acts on users’ assets
create.alsoAppP: The “app that handles users’ tokens” side (read / propose / authorize / deposit = authority & custody) will be covered in the full version. This dossier only covers the token you create.
create.scopeP: Detailed authority & custody (layer B), business model / value capture, tech choice (XRPL: MPT, escrow, delegation…), and non-EU jurisdictions.

configurator.title: Design by vector, not by decision tree
configurator.sub: Set a value on each axis, tick the functions and rights. The vector builds itself and coherence (constraints, forbidden cells) is checked live — then compared to 17 real-world cases.

principle.p1: A decision tree branches: each answer rules others out, you follow a path to a leaf. This grid doesn't branch — you set each axis independently, and the combination is the product. The tree qualifies; the grid designs.
principle.p2: Categorical axes (A1–A6) are mutually exclusive: one value. Flags (functions, rights) stack: a token is often a means of payment and a store of value and an investment at once.
principle.calloutP: The same word covers two roles with opposite consequences. We split them; the minter stays mere metadata.
principle.debtorP: Who owes you something → counterparty risk, redemption, insolvency. The “issuer” in the finance sense (a bond issuer, Circle for USDC).
principle.guarantorP: Who vouches → trust authority, liability for false attestation. The “issuer” in the W3C Verifiable Credentials sense (a university granting a diploma).
principle.example: A diploma has an identified guarantor but no debtor. Bitcoin has neither. A bond has both. Three real combinations → two distinct axes, not one.

configurator.constraintsSub: The grid isn't perfectly free: some combinations are contradictory, others reveal a legal boundary.

con.c1: native ⇒ coverage = n/a and debtor = none. “native + identified debtor” is contradictory: a native asset is a claim on no one.
con.c2: backed + pooled coverage + no debtor: a pooled claim implies a debtor — except the protocol case (debtor = diffuse, e.g. DAI).
con.c3: claim + no debtor + identified guarantor: allowed. A claim on a certified right doesn't imply a debt (carbon credit, authenticity NFT).
con.c4: attestation = yes ⇒ guarantor ≠ none. An attestation implies an attester.
con.c5: Pooled backing + simple redemption right → backed. Allocated/segregated asset with legal title → claim.

configurator.noMatch: No identical reference case on the axes set — potentially a new combination.

coherence.natif_couverture: native ⇒ coverage = n/a (a native asset represents no underlying)
coherence.natif_debiteur: native + a debtor is contradictory — a native asset is a claim on no one
coherence.mutualisee_sans_debiteur: pooled claim with no debtor ≈ empty case (except protocol → debtor = diffuse)
coherence.attestation_sans_garant: attestation ⇒ guarantor ≠ none (an attestation implies an attester)
coherence.titre_garant_valide: claim + identified guarantor with no debtor: valid (e.g. carbon credit, authenticity NFT)

configurator.librarySub: 17 real-world cases mapped onto the same axes. Filter and search, card or matrix view.
configurator.emptyLib: No case matches — empty or forbidden combination?

analyze.title: Analyze a token
analyze.sub: Describe an existing token; the same engine returns its likely class, watch points and what it would require — in diagnosis mode.
analyze.identSub: Describe what the token is. Unsure on a question? Pick “I don't know” — we'll take the prudent assumption and flag it.
analyze.condSub: How it's issued and distributed — what shapes the watch points and obligations.
analyze.banner: Diagnosis mode — read-only analysis of an existing token. Not legal advice; EU framework (MiCA / MiFID II).

build.title: Build an app
build.sub: An app that acts on users' assets. Map how much authority you take → risk profile, custody checklist, and the XRPL primitives to use.
build.questionsSub: Answer in plain terms — the power you take, its scope, duration, revocability, consent, and who holds the keys.
build.legal: Reminder: structuring, not legal advice. Authority/custody choices carry regulatory weight (MiCA custody / CASP) — validate with counsel. XRPL primitives are indicative; check amendment status before relying on them — e.g. Permission Delegation (XLS-75) is proposed, not yet live on mainnet.

login.title: Save your work
login.subtitle: Token Design works fully without an account. Sign in only to save and sync your dossiers across devices.
login.sentBody: We sent a sign-in link to {email}. Open it on this device to continue.
login.invalidEmail: Please enter a valid email address.
login.genericError: Something went wrong. Please try again.
login.localFirstHint: Local-first — your answers stay in this browser until you sign in.
login.walletHint: XRPL wallet (Crossmark, GemWallet, Xaman). No email — losing the wallet means losing the account.

cloud.signInToSave: Sign in to save this dossier to the cloud.
cloud.empty: No saved dossiers yet. Save one from any angle.

guideFrame.title: From answers to a regulatory class
guideFrame.lead: How the deterministic engine turns a few plain-language answers into a likely EU class, watch points and a launch checklist. Substance over form — never the technical standard. Not legal advice.
flowQ: Grants revenues, or a claim on a financial asset?
Stable value pegged to ONE official currency?
Pegged to a basket / a crypto?
guideFrame.s1body: We read the token's substance — fungibility, what it represents, who owes/attests, transferability, functions, rights — not its MPT/IOU/NFT form. Unanswered axes take the prudent assumption, tagged “to confirm”.
offNotes: Pays revenue / equity-like → MiFID II (prospectus, market approvals).
Genuinely unique → mostly outside MiCA (until series / fractionalization).
rules: rights include “revenues”  OR  claim on a financial underlying  →  Instrument
pegged to one official currency  →  EMT
pegged to a basket / crypto  →  ART
guideFrame.s3lead: Cumulative overlays that don't change the class but flag a boundary or a risk.
guideFrame.ratchets: Ratchets (points of no return): authorization-before-issuance · retail marketing · taking custody.

guideBuild.title: Power over users' assets → risk & custody
guideBuild.lead: For apps that act on users' tokens, the question isn't the token's class but how much authority you take over the wallet — with what reversibility, and who holds the keys. Not legal advice.
flowQ: Only builds transactions the user signs themselves?
Acts within limits the user delegated (user keeps the keys)?
guideBuild.s1body: “Issuer” isn't the question. Authority is: the more power you take over users' assets — and the less they can revoke it — the heavier your obligations (segregation, CASP/custody regimes).
guideBuild.xrplNote: XRPL has no ERC-20 blanket approve. Prefer native, ledger-enforced objects. Permission Delegation (XLS-75) is proposed, not yet live on mainnet.
